title: Support for HttpHeaders in containers-auth.json
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

When using `podman pull` with repo auth configured in ~/.config/containers/auth.json like this
```

{
    "auths": {
        "my.repo.loc": {
            "auth": "***"
        }
    },
    "HttpHeaders": {
        "User-Agent": "Guess-Who/1.0.0"
    }
}
```
podman sends user agent `libpod/3.0.1` not `Guess-Who/1.0.0`. Seems that `HttpHeaders` should be implemented in podman like in docker...

https://github.com/docker/cli/blob/master/man/docker-config-json.5.md

...and podman should not force own `User-Agent` header value if specified in auth.json (security reasons).

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.0.1
API Version:  3.0.0
Go Version:   go1.15.9
Built:        Thu Jan  1 01:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

comments.body: @vrothberg PTAL - will we need support for this in c/image?

Yes, that's something for c/image. Moving the issue.

@mtrmac, WDYT?

> ...and podman should not force own User-Agent header value if specified in auth.json (security reasons).

[I’m doubtful that this provides meaningful security, but, meh, firewalls and proxies and checklists.]

---

Yeah, this would be a c/image feature.

Right now callers can provide a `types.SystemContext.DockerRegistryUserAgent`, but of course if this should be configured in the default config files, it should be handled transparently by c/image.

One difficulty is that, on most Linux systems, `containers/auth.json` is gone after reboot, so it’s not a good place to have this configuration (in addition to `auth.json` being a weird name for a config file that specifies HTTP headers). We could implement it only as a `.docker/config.json`-exclusive compatibility feature, or introduce a new (per-registry??) option to `registries.conf`.

> I’m doubtful that this provides meaningful security

Don't limit users to define User-Agent, allow any other header like

`"X-You-Will-Love-Podman": "1"`

> containers/auth.json is gone after reboot

Not if located in ${XDG_CONFIG_HOME}/containers/auth.json and better to allow users to define HttpHeaders there for compatibility with docker.

> > I’m doubtful that this provides meaningful security
> 
> Don't limit users to define User-Agent, allow any other header like
> 
> `"X-You-Will-Love-Podman": "1"`

Yeah, that’s just public information / security by obscurity; or at best a shared secret between the client and whatever checks for this (unnecessary for a real registry that requires authentication, so I guess this targets some proxy on the way), strictly weaker than e.g. mutual TLS.

---

> > containers/auth.json is gone after reboot
> 
> Not if located in ${XDG_CONFIG_HOME}/containers/auth.json 

That’s not where `auth.json` is _intended_ to be located, for better or worse.

Sure, a system can be configured to store `auth.json` elsewhere, but we need to design the feature in a way that makes sense for users that use the intended default configuration.

> Yeah, that’s just public information / security by obscurity;

Think about users that don't want to advertise http client name and its version,

> we need to design the feature in a way that makes sense for users that use the intended default configuration

`HttpHeaders` should be defined in the same file where `Auth` is - both will produce http headers; don't reinvent the wheel and consider using dockers (sensible) solution if possible in podman.

No, credentials and configuration are quite different things.

We are just not redesigning the default location of `auth.json` to make configuring HTTP header settings more similar to Docker; the relative weight of the concerns does not warrant it, and we already have a precedent for `registries.conf:credential-helpers` BTW.

(I am not too strongly opposed to changing the default location of `auth.json`, it’s been a hassle for various reasons. But it’s an entirely different conversation, with hard tradeoffs WRT compatibility, and not a conversation that should block the HTTP header feature. We can implement the HTTP header option in `registries.conf` easily enough, but changing the ephemerality of `auth.json` is a thing that might never happen.)
I believe this should be stored in registries.conf if we do this.  But do you want the same default for Buildah, Podman and Skopoe,  Or should all of them be allowed to override it.

> I believe this should be stored in registries.conf if we do this

`HttpHeaders` should be client setting not registry setting (similar like in docker). Don't force users to redefine same User-Agent for all registries. You can alllow `HttpHeaders` specific for given registry also (to set headers only for connections to this registry) but this was not the intention of this issue.
